title: Controlling
summary: Controlling is measuring actual performance against planned standards and taking corrective action when deviations occur.
Controlling is the last function of management — it completes the management cycle and feeds back into planning.
Steps: Set standards → Measure performance → Compare actual vs standard → Analyse deviations → Take corrective action.
Planning and Controlling are inseparable twins: Planning sets targets, Controlling ensures they are met. Planning is looking ahead; Controlling is looking back.
Controlling is both backward-looking (analyses past performance) and forward-looking (improves future plans).
Importance: Accomplishing goals, Judging accuracy of standards, Making efficient use of resources, Improving motivation, Ensuring order and discipline.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: If the question says 'comparing actual with standard' → Controlling. 'Setting benchmarks' → could be Planning or first step of Controlling. The key difference: Planning = deciding what to do. Controlling = checking if it was done right. Remember: Controlling without planning is blind; Planning without controlling is meaningless.

definition.text: The management function of monitoring organisational performance towards the attainment of organisational goals, and taking corrective action when deviations occur.

sections.title: Importance of Controlling
points: 1. Accomplishing organisational goals: Keeps activities on track towards objectives.
2. Judging accuracy of standards: Helps verify if set standards were realistic.
3. Efficient use of resources: Ensures resources are not wasted — every activity contributes to goals.
4. Improving employee motivation: Employees know standards and work accordingly — fair evaluation motivates.
5. Ensuring order and discipline: Reduces dishonest behaviour through checks and audits.
6. Facilitating coordination: Each department's activities stay aligned with overall plans.

sections.title: Steps in the Controlling Process
points: 1. Setting Performance Standards: Benchmarks against which actual performance is measured. Should be measurable (units produced, revenue, etc.).
2. Measuring Actual Performance: Using personal observation, reports, statistical data, and accounting records.
3. Comparing Actual with Standards: Finding the deviation — actual minus standard. Deviations may be positive (exceeded) or negative (fell short).
4. Analysing Deviations: Management by Exception — focus on significant deviations, not every minor one. Use Critical Point Control — focus on key areas that matter most.
5. Taking Corrective Action: If deviations are within acceptable limits → no action needed. If significant → revise processes, retrain employees, or revise the standards themselves.

sections.title: Planning and Controlling — Inseparable Twins
points: Planning without controlling is meaningless — plans remain just intentions.
Controlling without planning is blind — no benchmarks to measure against.
Planning is prescriptive (what should be done); Controlling is evaluative (what was done).
Together they form a continuous cycle of improvement.

tables.title: Planning vs Controlling
rows: Deciding what to do in advance
Ensuring things are done as planned

questions.answer: The last function of management that completes the management cycle and feeds back into planning.

questions.text: Explain any three steps of the controlling process.
questions.answer:
(i) Setting Standards: Establishing benchmarks against which actual performance will be measured.
(ii) Measuring Actual Performance: Using observation, reports, and statistical data to assess what was achieved.
(iii) Taking Corrective Action: If deviations are significant, revising processes, retraining employees, or adjusting the standards.

questions.text: Explain the relationship between planning and controlling.
questions.answer:
Planning and controlling are inseparable twins:
(1) Planning sets the standards and goals; Controlling measures performance against those standards.
(2) Without planning, controlling is blind — there are no benchmarks to evaluate.
(3) Without controlling, planning is meaningless — plans remain just intentions.
(4) Planning is forward-looking (prescriptive); Controlling is backward-looking (evaluative).
(5) Controlling provides feedback that improves future plans — creating a continuous improvement cycle.
(6) Together they ensure organisational goals are achieved effectively and efficiently.

pyqs.text: State any three points of importance of controlling.
pyqs.answer:
(i) Accomplishing organisational goals by keeping activities on track.
(ii) Ensuring efficient use of resources by eliminating waste.
(iii) Improving employee motivation through fair evaluation against known standards.

related_chapters.title: Planning
related_chapters.reason: Planning and Controlling are inseparable twins
